questions.question: What is a security program?
options: A firewall OS running on dedicated hardware
An enterprise's set of security policies and procedures
A penetration-testing suite installed on endpoints
A software tool that manages user credentials
questions.explanation: A security program is an enterprise's set of security policies and procedures — covered inline in Jeremy's Day 48.

questions.question: Which program is designed to make employees aware of threats — for example, by sending fake phishing emails to test them?
options: User training program
User awareness program
Physical access control program
Incident response program
questions.explanation: User awareness programs make employees aware of potential threats, e.g. by sending fake phishing emails to test response.

questions.question: How does a user training program differ from a user awareness program?
options: Training is optional; awareness is mandatory
Training is more formal — dedicated sessions at onboarding and regular intervals
Training only covers passwords; awareness covers everything else
Training is for IT staff; awareness is for everyone else
questions.explanation: User training is more formal than awareness — dedicated sessions on security policies, strong passwords, and avoiding threats at onboarding and at regular intervals.

questions.question: What is physical access control designed to do?
options: Restrict Internet access to approved sites
Only allow authorized users into protected areas like network closets or data center floors
Filter Layer-2 frames on access ports
Encrypt data on removable media
questions.explanation: Physical access control protects equipment and data by only allowing authorized users into protected areas.

questions.question: A door lock that requires a badge swipe plus a fingerprint scan is an example of what?
options: Single-factor lock
Multifactor lock
Biometric-only lock
Smart-card-only lock
questions.explanation: A multifactor lock combines two factors — e.g. a badge swipe plus a fingerprint scan.

questions.question: Why is a badge system a useful physical-access control?
options: It is cheaper than physical keys
It provides a centralized, flexible way to grant and remove access
It eliminates the need for security cameras
It proves biometric identity by itself
questions.explanation: Badge systems provide a centralized, flexible way to grant and remove physical-access permissions — for example when an employee leaves the company.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical element of a security program covered in Jeremy's Day 48?
options: User awareness program
User training program
Physical access control
Quarterly earnings reporting
questions.explanation: Security program elements include user awareness, user training, and physical access control — financial reporting is not a security program element.
